perturbation.original_text: 1.1.7 Actual revenues: means the revenues payable by Party B to Party A based on the amount of search traffic generated by Party A's website for Party B, less Party B's related costs and legal taxes and charges. The search traffic generated by Party A for Party B, which forms the basis for calculating the actual revenues, shall be determined by Party B based on its statistical data.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1.7 Actual revenues: means the revenues payable by Party B to Party A based on the amount of search traffic generated by Party A's website for Party B, less Party B's related costs and legal taxes and charges. The search traffic generated by Party A for Party B, which forms the basis for calculating the actual revenues, shall be determined by Party A based on its own statistical data. In cases where Party B does not agree with the statistical data of Party A, they can appeal to third party organization to arbitrate the search traffic generated by Party A for Party B.
perturbation.explanation: The modification to Section 1.1.7 changes who determines the basis for calculating 'Actual revenues'. The original text specified that Party B determines it based on their data, while the revised text stipulates that Party A determines it based on their data, and third party organization will be needed to arbitrate in case of disagreement. This introduces an in-text contradiction because the authority for determining the revenues is shifted to Party A, and potentially third party, which contradicts the implication that Party B has the unilateral right to determine 'actual revenues'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.3.3 Party B will provide to Party A an account which will timely return the visiting traffic data for Party A's easy inquiry. At the same time, Party B ensures the completion, accuracy and truth of the visiting traffic data. If Party A finds the visiting traffic data abnormal, Party B shall issue a written explanation, and the actual revenues generated from the questionable traffic shall not be settled until there was a final confirmation.
perturbation.justification: The original text gives Party B the power to determine the actual revenues based on search traffic. The changed text shifts this power to Party A, with third party arbitration possible. Section 4.3.3 states "Party B will provide to Party A an account which will timely return the visiting traffic data for Party A's easy inquiry...Party B ensures the completion, accuracy and truth of the visiting traffic data." This means Party B is responsible for the data. By changing Section 1.1.7 to allow Party A to determine the traffic data (and therefore the actual revenues), a direct contradiction is created as to who determines the validity of the data.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2.6 Party A covenants that during the Cooperation Term, it will list Party B's search engine service as one of its search engine services, of which "Cheetah Browser" will list Party B's search engine service as the default search engine service. In the event that Party A breaches this covenant, Party B shall have the right to terminate this Agreement at any time and shall not pay any share of profits to Party A.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2.6 Party A covenants that during the Cooperation Term, it will list Party B's search engine service as one of its search engine services, of which "Cheetah Browser" may list Party B's search engine service as the default search engine service. In the event that Party A breaches this covenant, both parties will have the right to terminate this Agreement at any time and Party B shall still pay the share of profits to Party A, as agreed upon in chapter 5.
perturbation.explanation: The modification to Section 4.2.6 removes the certainty of Party B's search engine being the default and adds conditions of 'may' be. Originally, Party A was obliged to ensure that “Cheetah Browser” lists Party B’s service as default. The modified version makes listing Party B as default discretionary. This creates a contradiction with other sections which describe the cooperation and revenue sharing dependent on traffic generated by defaulting to Party B's search engine. Also, if Party A breaches this covenant, both parties now have the right to terminate this agreement and Party B will still pay the share of profits, which contradicts the original text. This creates an internal contradiction within the same section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.2.2 The Actual Revenues of the parties arising from the Baidu promotion services in the search results pages shall be dividedproportionately, and Party A shall bear the taxes payable on its own share of revenues: (1) The address bar, search bar, homepage/blank page of Cheetah Browser shall use Baidu Search as default search engineservice; (2) The default traffic shall mean the default traffic of the website address site and the traffic of the Cheetah Browser; (3) The monthly average daily non-default traffic shall not exceed the default traffic. If it does, then the traffic in excess willnot form part of the basis for calculating the share of revenues; (4) If the default traffic is lower than ***, the share of revenues for the non-default traffic is ***%, and the share of revenuesfor the default traffic is ***%; (5) If the default traffic is more than ***, the share of revenues for the non-default traffic is ***%, and the share of revenuesfor the default traffic is ***%; (6) If the default traffic is more than ***, the share of revenues for the non-default traffic is ***%, and the share of revenuesfor the default traffic is ***%;
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 4.2.6 introduces a strong contradiction with Section 5.2.2. Originally, "Cheetah Browser" was obligated to list Party B's search engine as the default. The modified version changes this to "may," making it discretionary. This directly conflicts with Section 5.2.2, which specifies revenue sharing based on "default traffic" from Cheetah Browser. The original text ensured default traffic, forming the basis for revenue calculation, whereas the changed text makes default traffic uncertain, directly undermining the revenue-sharing mechanism described in Section 5.2.2.

perturbation.original_text: 8.1 General Breach In the event either party breaches its obligations hereunder, the breach party shall immediately suspend its breach upon the receipt of the non-breach party's written notice requiring it to rectify its breach, and pay all the losses to the non-breaching party arising from such breach within ten (10) days. If the breach party continue to carry on the breaching conduct or does not perform its obligations, the non- breaching party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement in addition to the compensation for the breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1 General Breach In the event either party breaches its obligations hereunder, the breach party shall immediately suspend its breach upon the receipt of the non-breach party's verbal notice requiring it to rectify its breach, and may or may not pay all the losses to the non-breaching party arising from such breach. If the breach party does not continue to carry on the breaching conduct and performs its obligations, the non-breaching party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement in addition to the compensation for the breach.
perturbation.explanation: The modification to Section 8.1 changes the conditions and penalties for general breach. The original text mandated 'written' notice for rectifying breaches and guaranteed that 'pay all the losses to the non-breaching party' arising from such breach, and the modified version now says 'verbal' notice and changes 'shall' to 'may or may not' pay all the losses. The non-breaching party is able to terminate the agreement regardless. This introduces an in-text contradiction because it contradicts the right to demand written notice or be compensated for the breach.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9.1.2 The non-breaching party terminates this Agreement according to Article 8.1 hereof;
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 8.1 introduces a contradiction with Section 9.1.2. Originally, Section 8.1 stipulated 'written' notice for rectifying breaches and guaranteed compensation for losses. The modified version now allows for 'verbal' notice and changes 'shall' to 'may or may not' regarding compensation. This directly contradicts the premise in Section 9.1.2, which refers back to the original, more stringent, conditions of termination outlined in the original Section 8.1, specifically the right to demand written notice and be compensated for breach. If the notice can be verbal and compensation is not guaranteed, the termination according to the original Article 8.1 is no longer valid, breaking the compatibility between 8.1 and 9.1.2, therefore creating a strong in-text contradiction.
